label: Beginner · middlegame · played Rxd1 · picks Rxd1 over engine Qxd1

label: Beginner · middlegame · played Rd8 · picks Rd8 over engine Kc2

coach.coaching: I'd play Rd8. Your move was actually a sensible active choice: instead of only defending something, you moved the rook up to attack Black’s rook on the eighth rank. label: Advanced · endgame · played Rd5+

label: Advanced · middlegame · played Kh8

label: Advanced · opening · played Qb4

coach.coaching: ://www.chess.com/forum/view/endgame/advanced-chess-strategy-and-middlegame-plans I'd play Qe8. Your Qb4 was an active-looking queen move, but it missed the more useful consolidating role available here. The point of Qe8 is prophylactic: it reinforces both the pawn on **d7** and the rook behind it on **d8**, so you reduce tactical looseness before choosing whether to open the center later. It also keeps your structure flexible — if White continues calmly with something like **Ne2**, you have the clean regrouping **Nge7**, bringing another piece into coordination rather than forcing matters too early. How to find it: In positions like this, run a “before tension” check first: ask, 'If I make my natural attacking or centralizing move, what loose points am I leaving behind?' Here, notice that **Qe8** solves two defensive jobs at once by defending **d7/d8**, while still keeping future options such as ...d5 or ...Ba6 available after a quiet improving move like **Ne1-d2** followed by **Nge2-g7**. At this level, do not only look for activity — compare it against consolidation: if a quiet queen move improves safety without giving up your main plans, it should be seriously considered.

label: Advanced · opening · played Qg4

coach.coaching: I'd play Qe3. Your Qg4 was very understandable: it gets active by attacking both g7 and the bishop on H4 while also defending c4 and g2. The more instructive version is Qe3, because it solves several positional problems at once without becoming too committed. From e3, your queen still has pressure on e5, adds pressure on a7 via queenside tension, and importantly reinforces two defensive points: a3 and d2. That combination matters in an unbalanced middlegame: you want activity, but only if it improves coordination rather than creating new weaknesses later. How to find it: When your queen needs attention, do not start by asking “where can my queen attack?” Ask three questions in order: first, which queen moves keep useful targets? second, which ones defend something important? third, which one keeps flexibility after the opponent’s next improving move? Here, compare the jobs: Qg3→Qg4 creates direct contact with g7 and BH4, but Qe3 gives you central/eastern pressure on e2-e5-d2-c4-a3-b4 while keeping the queen flexible enough to meet ...Bg5 with Qe2. At this level, look for multi-purpose queen retreats before choosing the flashiest active-looking square.
coach.takeaway: With an exposed queen, prioritize safe squares that combine activity, defense, and future flexibility over immediate material chasing.

label: Advanced · opening · played f6

That matters because you improve coordination without loosening anything else. A useful sample direction is Nh6, and if White continues with Bf4, then ...Qh4+ keeps initiative by attacking the king on E1 while also putting pressure on H2. How to find it: In positions like this, run a “development first” filter before making a pawn move: ask, ‘Which of my pieces still has no role yet?’ Here, the knight on G8 needs development. Then ask, ‘Can I develop it to a square where it also fixes something important?’ Nh6 passes both tests: it develops the knight and reinforces f7. For advanced players, this is prophylaxis through activity — prevent future issues by activating a piece rather than spending a tempo on a structural commitment too early.
coach.takeaway: Before committing a pawn, look for a developing move that improves a passive piece and prevents a future target at the same time.

label: Intermediate · opening · played Qe2

How to find it: When there is no immediate loose piece to win, run this routine before making a queen move: ask, 'Can one of my knights enter the center and attack something important?' Check whether that central jump creates more than one problem for the opponent. Here, Ne4 passes the test because the knight improves, hits two targets at once — the bishop on B6/diagonal and queen on F7-diagonal — and still helps defend F2. That is usually stronger than simply moving the queen forward.
coach.takeaway: Before bringing the queen into action, look for a developing move that gains tempo by attacking multiple enemy pieces.
